transcript: My house is small. I like house.
Coffee good. I drink coffee morning.
I movie. Action movie fun.
Park near home. I walk park.
I live in an apartment. It has two rooms and a small kitchen.
I usually listen to pop music. I listen on the bus every morning.
My favorite cafe is near my office. The coffee is good and cheap.
I often walk in a park near my home. It is quiet, so I can relax after work.
Last weekend I watched a comedy with my friend. The story was simple, but we laughed a lot.
My room has a large desk by the window. I study there because it gets plenty of sunlight.
I started cooking at home last year because restaurant food was expensive. At first I made simple pasta, and now I can prepare several Korean dishes.
When I travel, I plan the hotel and transportation first. This helps me avoid stress, although I sometimes change my schedule during the trip.
My neighborhood changed recently. A new library opened, so more families visit the area and the streets feel much livelier than before.
I used to exercise alone at home, but I joined a gym this spring. Having a trainer keeps me motivated, and I follow a regular schedule three times a week.
A memorable trip was my visit to Busan. It rained on the first day, so we went to a museum instead of the beach and discovered an excellent local exhibition.
I compare several news sources every morning. Each source emphasizes different details, and checking them together helps me understand an issue more clearly.

Last month my flight was canceled because of heavy snow. I contacted the airline, explained that I had an important meeting, and asked for the earliest alternative. They moved me to a morning flight, so I informed my client and stayed at a nearby hotel.

The park in my neighborhood used to be an empty lot. After residents requested improvements, the city added walking paths, lights, and a playground. Now it is safer at night and has become a place where neighbors regularly meet.

I prefer working from home when I need to concentrate because I can control interruptions. However, for projects that require quick discussion, the office is more effective. I choose between them depending on the kind of work I have that day.

Streaming services have changed how I watch movies. In the past I selected from a limited theater schedule, whereas now I can explore international films whenever I want. The convenience is valuable, but I still visit theaters for films whose sound and visuals deserve a large screen.

During a team presentation, our main laptop suddenly stopped working. I divided the problem into two tasks: one colleague contacted technical support while I transferred the slides to a spare device. We started five minutes late, but the audience appreciated our calm explanation and the rest of the event went smoothly.

Public transportation in my city is more connected than it was ten years ago. New transfer stations reduced travel time, although crowded rush hours remain a problem. More frequent trains would make the system both efficient and comfortable.

Remote work offers flexibility, but its value depends on how a company manages communication. When expectations are vague, employees can feel isolated and decisions slow down. A hybrid policy works best for my team because focused tasks happen at home while scheduled office days preserve informal collaboration and shared context.

I once received a hotel room that was completely different from the reservation. Rather than simply demanding a refund, I showed the confirmation, described which features were essential for my family, and proposed two acceptable solutions. The manager found another room and waived the parking fee, turning a frustrating situation into a positive experience.

People often blame social media for unreliable news, yet the deeper issue is how quickly users react. I try to pause, locate the original source, and compare reporting from organizations with different perspectives. This routine takes a few minutes, but it prevents me from spreading claims that are emotionally persuasive and factually weak.

Urban development is frequently framed as a choice between economic growth and neighborhood identity, but that opposition is misleading. Cities can permit denser housing while preserving meaningful public spaces and locally significant buildings. The crucial step is involving residents early enough that consultation shapes the project instead of merely defending a decision already made.

Although artificial intelligence can personalize education, personalization alone does not guarantee learning. A system may efficiently reinforce a student's existing habits while failing to expose conceptual gaps. Teachers therefore need transparent evidence about why an activity was recommended and the authority to replace it when human observation contradicts the model.

When organizations encounter a public mistake, they often focus on minimizing immediate reputational damage. That instinct can produce vague apologies that satisfy neither customers nor employees. A credible response identifies the decision that failed, explains how incentives contributed to it, and establishes a measurable change whose progress can be examined later.

Tourism supports local employment, yet unmanaged popularity can erode the qualities that attracted visitors in the first place. Instead of imposing a single visitor cap, destinations can distribute demand across seasons, fund infrastructure through targeted fees, and give residents a formal role in deciding which forms of growth remain compatible with community life.

Convenience is often treated as an unquestionable benefit of digital services. However, each seamless interaction can conceal a transfer of responsibility from institutions to individuals, particularly when automated decisions are difficult to appeal. Good design should make routine tasks easy while preserving visible, accountable paths for exceptional circumstances.
